IMPACT.justification: The paper tackles the important end-to-end computational argumentation mining problem (relevant to legal, summarization and educational applications) and reports new state-of-the-art results on the Stab & Gurevych PE dataset, demonstrating practical gains. Its empirical finding that token-level BiLSTM-CRF/CNN tagging models outperform a dependency-parsing framing and that multi-task learning of natural subtasks improves performance provides a concrete reframing that is likely to change method choices in AM research. Because it uses widely applicable neural tagging and MTL techniques and evaluates on a standard corpus, the work is likely to be adopted and extended by others, opening follow-on work on neural end-to-end AM and related discourse tasks.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: The paper addresses computational argumentation mining using contemporary neural methods (BiLSTM-CRF, BiLSTM-CRF-CNN, LSTM-ER, multi-task learning) and evaluates on the established Persuasive Essays dataset (Stab & Gurevych, 2016) with the Persing & Ng (2016) metric, which aligns well with top-tier NLP standards. It compares against relevant baselines (feature-based ILP from Stab & Gurevych and parsing/tagging baselines), cites appropriate related work, and claims new state-of-the-art end-to-end results, making it well targeted to a top NLP/ML audience.

CLARITY.justification: The excerpt is poorly presented with extensive verbatim repetitions and duplicated paragraphs (e.g., multiple identical sentences about hand-crafted features and AM subtasks), which disrupt logical flow and organization. Main contributions are only briefly mentioned late rather than clearly stated early, notation/formatting is inconsistent (the F1 formula is malformed and repeated), and figures/tables are referenced but not shown or integrated; overall the writing contains grammatical/formatting issues that require substantial cleanup.

SUBSTANCE.justification: The experiments are broad and compare multiple framings and strong baselines (token-based dependency parsers MST/Mate and neural parsers, sequence taggers like BLCC, LSTM-ER, and the ILP feature-based system) on the Stab & Gurevych PE dataset (402 essays, 80 test) using the Persing & Ng evaluation metric, and they report both positive (tagging, MTL improvements) and negative (dependency parsing underperforms) results. Related work is extensively cited and the paper explicitly evaluates modular vs. end-to-end approaches and MTL setups. However, the text shows little evidence of systematic ablation or hyperparameter/sensitivity analyses (no detailed ablations of model components or training regimes), and reproducibility is limited because training details and exact model/hyperparameter settings are not given in the excerpt. Overall the empirical coverage is strong but misses some important analysis and reproducibility detail, hence a 4.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper states clear main claims (token-based parsing vs. sequence tagging, MTL helps, and new state-of-the-art on the PE dataset) and uses appropriate baselines/dataset (Stab & Gurevych PE corpus, ILP feature baseline, LSTM-ER, BiLSTM-CRF/BLCC, dependency parsers) and a standard evaluation metric (Persing & Ng). However, the provided text contains no quantitative results, ablation details, training/hyperparameter settings or statistical significance tests to verify the SOTA and other performance claims. There are no mathematical derivations to check, but some assertions (e.g., that BiLSTMs capture long-range dependencies sufficiently or that learned models internalize dataset constraints) are not supported by presented analyses, creating a gap between claims and shown evidence.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper mainly adapts and combines existing neural methods (BiLSTM-CRF/BLCC, LSTM-ER from Miwa & Bansal 2016, MTL schemes from Søgaard & Goldberg 2016, neural parsers) to the end-to-end AM task rather than proposing fundamentally new architectures. Its noteworthy elements are the framings it evaluates (token-based dependency parsing vs. token-level sequence tagging with distances encoded into tag labels) and empirical findings that BiLSTM tagging and multi-task training outperform dependency-parser framings on the PE dataset. Overall this is an incremental, application-driven contribution with some novel problem framing and empirical insights but no major methodological innovation.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: The paper compares against appropriate and recent baselines: it evaluates the feature-based ILP system of Stab and Gurevych (2016) and Persing and Ng (2016) metric on the same PE dataset, adapts and tests LSTM-ER (Miwa & Bansal, 2016), BLCC taggers (Ma & Hovy, 2016) and several parser variants (MST, Mate, Dyer, Kiperwasser). Comparisons appear fair (same dataset and Persing & Ng evaluation metric) and the manuscript analyzes differences between framings (token-based dependency parsing vs BiLSTM tagging, and multi-task setups), reporting that tagging + MTL yields better results and claiming new state-of-the-art on PE. The paper also cites and discusses the most relevant competing approaches and prior work throughout (e.g., Persing & Ng, Stab & Gurevych, Peldszus & Stede, Potash et al.).
